figures.caption: FIG. 2. Detecting interface piezoelectric qubit-phonon coupling. a, Pulse sequence used to probe the effect of electromechanical coupling at a fixed delay time tdelay = τ0 = 3 µs following qubit excitation. b, Simulated electric-field distribution generated by applying Vbias = 1 V to the XY line. Scale bar represents 30 µm. c, The standard tunneling model of a TLS. An applied voltage bias Vbias shifts the TLS transition energy ℏωTLS. d, Qubit excited state population pe measured at delay time τ0 as a function of qubit frequency fq and TLS-tuning bias voltage Vbias. e, Bias-averaged pe at fixed delay time τ0, revealing the spectral signatures of the SAW resonator modes. f, Reference transmission through a pair of IDTs with identical dimensions on a separate device shows the operational bandwidth of the transducers (Sample D).

FIG. 3. Qubit lifetime reduction due to piezoelectric coupling to SAW resonator modes. a, Pulse sequence used for coherence measurements. b, Example of an energy-relaxation (T1) characterization measured at fq =3.14 GHz. c, Example of a Ramsey (T ∗ 2 ) characterization measured at fq =3.14 GHz. d, Bias-averaged T ∗ 2 as a function of qubit frequency. The decreasing trend of T ∗ 2 away from the sweet spot is captured by the flux sensitivity T ∗ 2 ∝ (∂fq/∂Iflux) −1 [20]. e, Bias-averaged T1 as a function of qubit frequency fq. The white-background region indicates the bandwidth of the SAW mirror. f, Zoomed-in view of panel e, fitted using Eq. (2). The fit yields the background relaxation time T (0) 1 , indicated by the gray dashed line, as well as the electromechanical coupling strength gm,k and the SAW resonator mode linewidth κm,k. (See Table. S2.) Inset: optical micrograph of Sample A. Lshort = 380 µm is the distance between the two sets of reflectors. g, Same as panel f, but for Sample B, which has a larger resonator length Llong than Sample A. Inset: optical micrograph of Sample B. Llong = 1120 µm is the distance between the two sets of reflectors.
